title: Democratic Representation and Local Voice
content:
What recent experience shows

Reorganisation reshapes the relationship between elected members, communities and decision making. The move to larger unitary authorities generally results in fewer councillors representing larger populations and wider geographic areas.

Councils report increased casework demands on individual members, reduced visibility and accessibility for some communities, and greater reliance on town and parish councils to maintain local connection. Some authorities have sought to mitigate these effects through locality arrangements, including area committees, delegated budgets and strengthened parish engagement.

Despite these measures, concerns about democratic distance and reduced local influence remain a persistent feature of post-reorganisation feedback.

What this tells us

Democratic capacity does not automatically scale with organisational size. Without deliberate design, larger authorities risk weakening the connection between residents and decision makers, particularly in areas with strong local identities or dispersed communities.

What this means for future reorganisations

Future reorganisations should treat democratic representation and locality as core design issues. Local engagement mechanisms, member capacity and the role of parish and town councils should be addressed explicitly from the outset, rather than relying on informal or retrospective solutions.
